markdown:
# Comparing the Original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2018)

Below is a side-by-side comparison grounded in the two source papers.

---

## 1. Architecture Role

**Transformer — full encoder–decoder sequence transduction model.**
Vaswani et al. position it explicitly within the standard encoder–decoder paradigm: the encoder maps an input sequence to continuous representations z = (z₁,…,zₙ), and the decoder then generates an output sequence (y₁,…,yₘ) one element at a time [1](attention_is_all_you_need_2017/reading_element_c4c19cd7893248e68718b34c655eb76f). The architecture "follows this overall architecture using stacked self-attention and point-wise, fully connected layers for both the encoder and decoder, shown in the left and right halves of Figure 1" [2](attention_is_all_you_need_2017/reading_element_aa69509b4243455da15d24dbfae670a7).

- **Encoder** (N=6 layers): multi-head self-attention + position-wise feed-forward, with residual connections and layer norm [3](attention_is_all_you_need_2017/reading_element_03dba0820f6e439d976b2a4bf51178ef).
- **Decoder** (N=6 layers): same two sub-layers plus a third sub-layer doing multi-head attention over the encoder stack; self-attention is "modified to prevent positions from attending to subsequent positions" [4](attention_is_all_you_need_2017/reading_element_adce10960c0b4a1dac2bcb0ce5312d34).

**BERT — encoder-only.**
Devlin et al. state: "BERT's model architecture is a multi-layer bidirectional Transformer encoder based on the original implementation described in Vaswani et al. (2017) … our implementation is almost identical to the original" [5](bert_2018/reading_element_3f7beee8c6f3400d81fdce44c3bae795). The name itself decodes this: **B**idirectional **E**ncoder **R**epresentations from **T**ransformers. There is no decoder stack; instead "the pre-trained BERT model can be finetuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks" [6](bert_2018/reading_element_a5c22e58afbb4dc09f9cdcf3b5b800f4).

| | Transformer | BERT |
|---|---|---|
| Role in pipeline | Encoder–decoder sequence generator | Standalone encoder for representation / fine-tuning |
| Output head | Built-in decoder + softmax projection [7](attention_is_all_you_need_2017/reading_element_0ca2035128c44f6ea0f60b5502fb57af) | Task-specific layer added at fine-tune time [8](bert_2018/reading_element_0871f142ab20436cb7b829b983bff573) |
| Decoding | Auto-regressive token-by-token generation [1] | No generation; representation extraction only |

---

## 2. Directionality

The two models occupy opposite ends of the spectrum within the same Transformer family.

**Transformer — mixed / partial directionality.**
- **Encoder self-attention is fully bidirectional.** A standard multi-head self-attention layer attends over all positions in the input sequence.
- **Decoder self-attention is left-to-right (auto-regressive).** Vaswani et al. "modify the self-attention sub-layer in the decoder stack to prevent positions from attending to subsequent positions. This masking, combined with fact that the output embeddings are offset by one position, ensures that the predictions for position i can depend only on the known outputs at positions less than i" [4](attention_is_all_you_need_2017/reading_element_adce10960c0b4a1dac2bcb0ce5312d34). So during training and decoding, the model consumes "the previously generated symbols as additional input when generating the next" [1](attention_is_all_you_need_2017/reading_element_c4c19cd7893248e68718b34c655eb76f).
- **Encoder–decoder cross-attention** lets every decoder position attend over the *complete* encoder output (full source-side context) [[attention_is_all_you_need_2017 §3.2.3]].

**BERT — purely bidirectional, jointly conditioned.**
BERT is introduced as "a new language representation model … designed to pretrain deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers" [6](bert_2018/reading_element_a5c22e58afbb4dc09f9cdcf3b5b800f4). Devlin et al. motivate the choice explicitly: "standard conditional language models can only be trained left-to-right or right-to-left, since bidirectional conditioning would allow each word to indirectly 'see itself,' and the model could trivially predict the target word in a multi-layered context" — which is why they turn to masked LM rather than vanilla CLM [9](bert_2018/reading_element_ac5053fcd03b41ea8d3af99e56c106b7). The appendix figure reinforces: "BERT uses a bidirectional Transformer. OpenAI GPT uses a left-to-right Transformer. ELMo uses the concatenation of independently trained left-to-right and right-to-left LSTMs … Among the three, only BERT representations are jointly conditioned on both left and right context in all layers" [10](bert_2018/figure-152).

Net effect: in Transformer, attention directionality is *deliberately split* between encoder and decoder; in BERT, the entire network is unconstrained in both directions.

---

## 3. Training Objective

**Transformer — supervised sequence-to-sequence (translation) with auto-regressive next-token prediction.**
The model is trained on parallel data for machine translation (WMT 2014 En–De and En–Fr) using an auto-regressive decoder objective: a linear projection over the decoder output converted via the "usual learned linear transformation and softmax function … to predicted next-token probabilities" [7](attention_is_all_you_need_2017/reading_element_0ca2035128c44f6ea0f60b5502fb57af). Training uses the Adam optimizer (β₁=0.9, β₂=0.98, ε=10⁻⁹) with a warmup-linear-then-inverse-sqrt-decay learning-rate schedule [11](attention_is_all_you_need_2017/reading_element_260d1b8af78b454dbe0f610483436730). Regularization includes label smoothing ε_ls = 0.1 — "this hurts perplexity, as the model learns to be more unsure, but improves accuracy and BLEU score" [12](attention_is_all_you_need_2017/reading_element_a576f0d28cc640b7aa8330b3b8a1edbe).

**BERT — self-supervised pre-training with Masked LM + Next Sentence Prediction, followed by task fine-tuning.**
Two pre-training tasks are jointly optimized:

1. **Masked Language Modeling (MLM).** "The masked language model randomly masks some of the tokens from the input, and the objective is to predict the original vocabulary id of the masked word based only on its context. Unlike left-to-right language model pre-training, the MLM objective enables the representation to fuse the left and the right context, which allows us to pretrain a deep bidirectional Transformer" [13](bert_2018/reading_element_db79b2580f2a4f8a8a09ffcfeca354ee). Concretely, 15% of token positions are chosen; the chosen token is replaced with `[MASK]` 80% of the time, a random token 10%, or left unchanged 10%; the model then predicts the original token with cross-entropy loss [14](bert_2018/reading_element_f6e32313b401438fa46be4a39c1540fc).

2. **Next Sentence Prediction (NSP).** A "binarized next sentence prediction task … 50% of the time B is the actual next sentence that follows A (labeled as IsNext), and 50% of the time it is a random sentence from the corpus (labeled as NotNext)" [15](bert_2018/reading_element_3d4803f42a3f44c6b3898d3f357e7ae9).

After pre-training, "a simple classification layer is added to the pre-trained model, and all parameters are jointly fine-tuned on a downstream task" (a feature-based variant is also discussed) [8](bert_2018/reading_element_0871f142ab20436cb7b829b983bff573).

| Objective aspect | Transformer | BERT |
|---|---|---|
| Supervision signal | Parallel translation data (supervised) | Unlabeled monolingual text (self-supervised) |
| Prediction target | Next token in target sequence (auto-regressive) | (1) Original vocabulary id of masked tokens; (2) IsNext / NotNext for sentence pairs |
| Loss surface | Cross-entropy on next-token probabilities with label smoothing ε_ls=0.1 | Cross-entropy on masked-token logits + binary classification on NSP |
| Usage after training | Directly used for generation | Pre-training stage; weights transferred and fine-tuned on downstream tasks |

---

## Summary

| Dimension | Original Transformer | BERT |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture role | Encoder + decoder for end-to-end sequence transduction | Encoder-only stack used as a representation model |
| Directionality | Encoder: bidirectional; Decoder: masked left-to-right (auto-regressive) | Bidirectional at every layer, jointly seeing left and right context |
| Training objective | Supervised next-token prediction over a target sequence with cross-entropy + label smoothing | Self-supervised MLM + NSP on unlabeled text, followed by task fine-tuning |

Together, the comparison shows that BERT inherits the *encoder half* of the original Transformer, opens up its attention fully (bidirectionality), and replaces the supervised translation objective with denoising-style self-supervised pretraining objectives that can later be transferred to many tasks via fine-tuning.

Sources
[1] Attention Is All You Need, 3 Model Architecture, p. 2
[2] Attention Is All You Need, 3 Model Architecture, p. 3
[3] Attention Is All You Need, 3.1 Encoder and Decoder Stacks, p. 3
[4] Attention Is All You Need, 3.1 Encoder and Decoder Stacks, p. 3
[5] BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, 3 BERT, p. 3
[6] BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, Abstract, p. 1
[7] Attention Is All You Need, 3.4 Embeddings and Softmax, p. 5
[8] BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, 5.3 Feature-based Approach with BERT, p. 9
[9] BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, 3.1 Pre-training BERT, p. 4
[10] BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, A.1 Illustration of the Pre-training Tasks, p. 13
[11] Attention Is All You Need, 5.3 Optimizer, p. 7
[12] Attention Is All You Need, 5.4 Regularization, p. 8
[13] BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, 1 Introduction, p. 1
[14] BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, 3.1 Pre-training BERT, p. 4
[15] BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, 3.1 Pre-training BERT, p. 4
